channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
item.description: It is the centerpiece of President Trump’s drive to make more chips in the United States, but the company still has a long way to go before it can be called a complete turnaround.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Lincoln Agnew

item.title: The High School Pipeline to South Korea’s Chip-Making Fortunes
item.description: Huge memory-chip profits from the global A.I. boom have increased interest in semiconductor factory work. But behind the hype are uncertain job prospects.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Tina Hsu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A class about diffusion equipment operation taught by Kang Soo Jin.

item.title: Stocks in Asia Tumble as A.I. Uncertainty Jolts Tech Shares
item.description: Stock markets across Asia tumbled, as investors continue to wrestle with whether the boom in artificial intelligence spending is overdone or just beginning.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Greg Baker/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
item.description: The A.I. company’s advisers are pushing its chief executive, Sam Altman, to move slowly after SpaceX’s stock has been volatile and as the start-up grapples with financial challenges.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, the chief executive of OpenAI, has pushed for a trillion-dollar I.P.O.

item.title: Movement Through Strait of Hormuz Is Halted After Attack on Cargo Ship
item.description: The strike on a vessel near the coast of Oman prompted a U.N. agency to pause an evacuation of ships from the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, early this month.

item.title: Medical Journal Retracts Study Claiming Cancer Therapy Is More Effective When Given in the Morning
item.description: In a notice flagging a series of problems with a clinical trial, the journal Nature Medicine said its editors “no longer have confidence in the integrity of the results.”
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: The journal Nature Medicine retracted a study that found a drastic benefit when cancer therapy was administered in the morning.

item.title: SpaceX IPO Shows How Investors in Public Companies Are Losing Power
item.description: SpaceX has joined a growing cohort of companies that use dual class share structures to ensure many shareholders have little say in how they are run.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni and Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bret Johnsen, chief financial officer of SpaceX, celebrates with investors and company members at the Nasdaq the day of the company’s initial public offering.

item.title: Chemours to Pay $450M in First Federal PFAS Settlement
item.description: The settlement addresses the dumping of PFAS “forever chemicals,” which have been linked to cancer and other risks, by Chemours in several states.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Ed Kashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Chemours plant in Fayetteville, N.C. Under a proposed settlement, Chemours would pay fines for discharging PFAS from plants in North Carolina, New Jersey and West Virginia.

item.title: Apple Raises Prices on Macs and iPads Amid the A.I. Boom
item.description: The tech giant cited the soaring costs of memory and storage chips as it increased prices more than $200 on some devices.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Even with a sprawling supply chain, Apple is not immune to the global shortage of chips.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Against Bayer Alleging Roundup Weedkiller Caused Cancer
item.description: The court’s decision is likely to determine the future of thousands of lawsuits against Bayer, which manufactures the weedkiller, over similar claims.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Josh Edelson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Roundup, which was created in the 1970s, is one of the most popular weedkillers in the world.

item.title: Marianne Lake, a Potential Dimon Successor, Leaves JPMorgan
item.description: Another potential successor, Marianne Lake, is leaving JPMorgan, as the longstanding chief executive enters his third decade atop the bank.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:credit: Carly Zavala for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan Chase’s chief executive since 2006, has said the plan for his successor is “up to God and the board.”

item.title: Inflation Accelerated in May as Iran War Pushed Up Prices
item.description: A closely watched measure of inflation ticked up as the conflict in the Middle East inflated energy prices.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Gasoline prices are up more than 30 percent since the start of the war in Iran.

item.title: Iran Strikes Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Undermining Efforts to Restore Traffic
item.description: The attack, confirmed by U.S. and Iranian officials, came after Tehran threatened to disrupt shipping in the waterway and as Washington sought regional support for its peace agreement.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Farnaz Fassihi, Jenny Gross and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels off the coast of Oman near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday.

item.title: The Taylor Swift Wedding and a Hot New York Summer
item.description: Should Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce tie the knot in New York City, their nuptials would be another economic event for the Big Apple.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Ashley Landis/Associated Press
item.media:description: New York and fans of Taylor Swift are buzzing over the prospect of Swift and Travis Kelce getting married in the city.

item.title: Larry Sanger, a Wikipedia Founder, Is Barred From Editing on the Site
item.description: A vote to bar Larry Sanger indefinitely from the volunteer community of Wikipedia came days after he submitted a proposal on “intellectual diversity.”
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Ulli Winkler/ullstein bild, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Larry Sanger said that Wikipedia was “one of the most effective organs of establishment propaganda in history.”

item.title: Big Companies Aim to Ease A.I. Transition for American Workers
item.description: OpenAI, Anthropic, Amazon and Microsoft have signed on to an effort led by Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary and Rhode Island governor, is calling for companies and the government to do more to orient American workers in a new A.I. era.

item.title: Lesson Learned From Iran War: Reduce Reliance on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Increased stockpiles, more pipelines and other tactics could loosen the waterway’s stranglehold on the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A vessel in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, this week.

item.title: U.S. Inflation Problems Are Far From Over
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s new chairman has vowed to deliver price stability, but officials are at odds over whether that will require higher borrowing costs.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the new chairman of the Federal Reserve, has expressed interest in examining how it measures and models price pressures.

item.title: How Honda’s Pledge to Go All-Electric Unraveled
item.description: A derailed gamble led Honda to its first-ever annual loss and has intensified scrutiny over the future of its chief executive.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: John Locher/Associated Press
item.media:description: Honda was developing several next-generation electric vehicles that were ultimately scrapped because of declining global E.V. demand.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After Attack Halts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Oil prices fell, moving closer to levels not seen since the war in Iran began in February.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images

item.title: She Stole a Knicks Trash Can Off the Street and Lost Her Job at JPMorgan
item.description: After a video of Angie Baez taking a Knicks-themed trash can went viral, a JPMorgan Chase spokeswoman said she was no longer with the company.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman and Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Knicks’ championship parade last week in Manhattan.

item.title: With a Proposed $250 Bill, Trump Reimagines America by Putting Himself on Its Money
item.description: Cash might be a relic, but — like royalty and emperors — President Trump still wants his face on American currency.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport

item.title: Norma Yaeger, Barrier-Breaking Stockbroker, Dies at 96
item.description: Over her husband’s objections, she became a Wall Street broker in the 1960s. She later started two securities firms in the overwhelmingly male financial world.
item.dc:creator: James R. Hagerty

item.title: Airbus Is Ordered to Inspect 16 Jets After Cracks Are Found in Wings
item.description: The European Union’s chief aviation regulator ordered five A380 superjumbo jets to be grounded immediately.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Eric Piermont/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: An Airbus A380 flying at Paris-Le Bourget Airport in 2017.

item.title: Why the Fed Could Loom Large Over the Midterms
item.description: Wall Street increasingly predicts the central bank will raise interest rates, possibly ahead of Election Day in November. That could anger President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The odds are rising on Wall Street that the Fed will raise interest rates, perhaps before Election Day.

item.title: Housing Package Passed by Congress Has Wide Appeal, but It’s No Quick Fix
item.description: The first housing package in decades will take time to put into effect, testing the patience of families rankled by high prices.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which cleared Congress on Tuesday, aims to increase new construction.

item.title: Your Home Could Help Solve AI’s Growing Power Demand
item.description: Tesla, Sunrun and Renew Home plan to tap solar panels, batteries, thermostats and other devices installed in millions of homes to meet the energy demands of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Collin Chappelle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Installation of a Sunrun solar panel on a roof in California. Sunrun is working with Tesla and Renew Home to use home energy devices to help data centers get online faster.

item.title: Another Spirit of ’76: George Washington’s Whiskey
item.description: A lesser-known legacy of the first president is his Mount Vernon distillery, whose return has helped revive an interest in American whiskey.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Scott Suchman for The New York Times

item.title: Welcome to the Luxury City Built by Taiwan’s A.I. Boom
item.description: Fortunes, luxury buildings and birthrates are rising in the city at the center of Taiwan’s chip supply chain.
item.dc:creator: Amy Chang Chien and Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The shopping mall in Hsinchu, called Big City, ranks among Taiwan’s top retail centers.

item.title: U.S. World Cup Cities Are on a Counterdrone Spending Spree
item.description: FEMA has given cities and states $250 million to protect the World Cup from airborne threats. That equipment will remain in place after the tournament.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times

item.title: A.I. Riches Fuel Economic Divide in Asia’s Chip Powerhouses
item.description: A.I. demand is driving stock market gains and booming exports in South Korea and Taiwan. But the rest of the economy is being left behind.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: An Rong Xu for The New York Times

item.title: What’s Behind Broadway’s New Musical Drought?
item.description: Hollywood actors in starry plays, skittish investors and gate-keeping theater owners have all contributed to an unusually tough climate for song-and-dance shows.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (Play)
Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil (Play)
item.media:credit: Tim Lahan

item.title: Alibaba Sues Pentagon Over China Military Label
item.description: The Chinese tech giant said it had no ties to China’s military and that the U.S. government had violated the law by making that claim.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alibaba has become one of China’s leading artificial intelligence companies.

item.title: Oil Prices Drop on Moves to Unblock Persian Gulf Crude Supplies
item.description: Oil prices
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: David Zalubowski/Associated Press

item.title: Trump’s Pick for Top I.R.S. Lawyer Works at Firm That Represents Him
item.description: James R. Gadwood, the president’s nominee for chief counsel at the Internal Revenue Service, works at Miller & Chevalier, which has represented Mr. Trump in tax matters.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Internal Revenue Service building in Washington. James R. Gadwood is the second person President Trump has nominated to the agency’s top legal job in his second term.

item.title: U.S. Presses Meta to Agree to A.I. Reviews
item.description: Federal officials are urging the lone major tech company holdout to allow government safety evaluations, weeks after ordering Anthropic to pull its latest model.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Eli Tan and Sheera Frenkel
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta released its most recent artificial intelligence model in April.

item.title: Apple Just Closed Its First Unionized U.S. Store
item.description: The company said it had shuttered a Maryland location because of “declining conditions” in a surrounding mall. The union accuses Apple of retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.category: International Assn of Machinists and Aerospace Workers
item.media:credit: Matt Roth for The New York Times
item.media:description: A FedEx employee removed stock from an Apple Store on Monday in Towson, Md.

item.title: SpaceX Stock Price Is Slumping After Its Blockbuster IPO
item.description: On Tuesday, SpaceX briefly fell below $150 a share, which was where the stock began trading when the company listed publicly just over a week ago.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Trump Gives Teamsters a Chance to Shed Oversight Meant to Curb Mob Ties
item.description: Sean M. O’Brien, re-elected to a second term leading the union, has used a relationship with President Trump to end court-ordered corruption monitoring.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sean M. O’Brien, the Teamsters president, was overwhelmingly re-elected to his second term at the union’s convention last week.

item.title: CBS News’s Independent Watchdog Stays Quiet Amid ‘60 Minutes’ Turmoil
item.description: With CBS News rocked by controversy, some viewers have turned to the network ombudsman for a public response. So far, they haven’t gotten one.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Michael Brochstein/SOPA Images, via LightRocket, via Getty Images
item.media:description: CBS News appointed Kenneth R. Weinstein, shown in 2024, as its ombudsman in September under an agreement with the Federal Communications Commission.

item.title: The Iran War’s Persistent Threat to Farmers in Poor Countries
item.description: A reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would do little to swiftly ease the pain inflicted by higher prices for fertilizer, food and fuel in Ivory Coast.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman and Finbarr O’Reilly
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Justice Dept. Unveils Medicare Fraud Charges as Part of Larger Health Care Prosecutions
item.description: The department is taking aim at pricey “skin substitutes,” an overused wound care treatment that cost the government nearly $15 billion in 2025.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff
item.media:credit: Ken Cedeno/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Mehmet Oz, center, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, with Todd Blanche, right, the acting attorney general, and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. during a briefing at the Justice Department on Tuesday.

item.title: Strait Reopens and Iranian Oil Flows, But the Path Through Is Complicated
item.description: With the central route of the Strait of Hormuz laden with mines, ships are taking the northern route in Iranian waters or the southern path in Omani waters. Both have risks.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Crude oil tankers, bulk carriers and vessels anchored in Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: The Tech Sell-off Goes Global
item.description: Investors are bracing for rough trading after stocks in Asia and Europe were clobbered on Tuesday. A.I. companies like SpaceX are getting hit hard.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Jade Gao/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The tech sell-off has gone global on Tuesday.

item.title: Markets Recoil in Global Sell-Off Driven by Tech Stocks
item.description: Chip makers led the way down in South Korea, where the main index plunged 10.5 percent. The Nasdaq in the U.S. was down 2.2 percent.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi, Jason Karaian and Aruni Soni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jade Gao/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Traders in Seoul. The decline in South Korean stocks on Tuesday at one point triggered a 20-minute trading halt by the exchange operator.

item.title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
item.description: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was declared the world’s fastest. It uses only standard microprocessors and not the special-purpose chips called graphics processing units.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:credit: Xu Congjun/VCG, via Getty Images
item.media:description: China’s National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi in 2020. LineShine, a supercomputer in Shenzhen, has been declared the world’s fastest.

item.title: From Florence to Malta, 5 New Boutique Hotels
item.description: Whether set amid vineyards in Burgundy or in the heart of Florence, these boutique hotels will keep the crowds at bay.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: America’s Thirst for Gasoline May Not Recover After Iran War
item.description: People drove less and bought more-efficient cars when fuel prices surged, habits that could stick over the long term.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: When gas prices started rising, Judy Vassallo, 89, started taking the city bus, which is free for seniors.

item.title: As Chinese Tech Pulls Ahead, U.S. Fears It Will Become Dependent
item.description: Chinese firms have some of the world’s most advanced technology. But U.S. officials say relying on it could come with a downside.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times

item.title: Those British Strawberries Are Being Picked by Central Asian Workers
item.description: Ten years after Brexit, most seasonal workers in Britain are from countries such as Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan. Without them, agricultural chiefs say, many farms would fail.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Aigerim Turgunbaeva
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shukrat Djuraev at Homefield Farm, in Kent, England, this month.
